content.problemStatement:
Chef and Divyam are playing a game with the following rules:


First, an integer X!X!X! is written on a board.
Chef and Divyam alternate turns; Chef plays first.
In each move, the current player should choose a positive integer DDD which is divisible by up to YYY distinct primes and does not exceed the integer currently written on the board. Note that 111 is not a prime.
DDD is then subtracted from the integer on the board, i.e. if the integer written on the board before this move was AAA, it is erased and replaced by A−DA-DA−D.
When one player writes 000 on the board, the game ends and this player wins.
Given XXX and YYY, determine the winner of the game.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers X and Y.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Chef" if Chef wins the game, otherwise "Divyam".

sampleTestCases.explanation: π(1)=0 ≤2 → Chef can take 1! =1 and win.\nπ(3)=2 >1 → 3! has two distinct primes, not allowed; first player loses.\nπ(2021)=306 >42 → Divyam wins.

solution.approach:
The integer written on the board at the start is X!.
The only property of a move that matters is the number of distinct prime factors of the chosen D. Let ω(D) be that number. A move is allowed iff ω(D) ≤ Y.
Observe that ω(X!) equals the number of distinct primes not larger than X, i.e. π(X). If π(X) ≤ Y then X! itself satisfies the move condition, so Chef can subtract the whole X! in his first turn and win immediately.
If π(X) > Y, then X! contains at least Y+1 distinct primes. Any allowed D can contain at most Y distinct primes, therefore after subtracting D the remaining number is still positive and still has at least one of those Y+1 primes as a factor. Consequently the opponent can always mirror the situation and eventually force a win for the second player. Hence the starting position is losing for the first player.
Thus the winner is decided solely by comparing π(X) with Y.
Implementation steps:
1. Pre‑compute an array primeCnt[i] = number of primes ≤ i for all i up to 10^6 using a linear/Eratosthenes sieve (O(N log log N)).
2. For each test case read X, Y and output "Chef" if primeCnt[X] ≤ Y else "Divyam".
The algorithm runs in O(N log log N + T) time and O(N) memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log log N + T), where N = 10^6 (pre‑computation) and T is the number of test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the sieve and prefix array.

code.Python3:
import sys
MAX = 10**6
is_prime = bytearray(b'\x01') * (MAX+1)
is_prime[0] = is_prime[1] = 0
for p in range(2, int(MAX**0.5)+1):
    if is_prime[p]:
        step = p
        start = p*p
        is_prime[start:MAX+1:step] = b'\x00' * ((MAX - start)//step + 1)
prime_cnt = [0]*(MAX+1)
cnt = 0
for i in range(2, MAX+1):
    if is_prime[i]:
        cnt += 1
    prime_cnt[i] = cnt

data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
if not data:
    sys.exit()
it = iter(data)
T = int(next(it))
out_lines = []
for _ in range(T):
    X = int(next(it)); Y = int(next(it))
    out_lines.append('Chef' if prime_cnt[X] <= Y else 'Divyam')
sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))
